replies.author: @Xoorbie
replies.text:
@Midwinter2 thanks for the recommendation. I will check it out. And I am not doubting his abilities as an actor. I am admittedly not familiar with his acting. My concern with his portrayal of Snape has to do with immersion. A different comment I posted illustrates my concern: I don’t care how good of an actor this man is. He is unqualified for the role by virtue of his appearance. Like it or not, and for better or for worse, our appearance is our first form of communication. Because we are not viewing this man on the street, and are instead viewing him in a very well-established and beloved IP, the first thing he’s communicating to anyone familiar with the universe is that he is NOT Snape. That is a big problem. High quality/effective entertainment is dependent on effective immersion.   I am not immersed. I am in fact the opposite of immersed: disrupted, distracted, confused and irritated. 

** Note: I am speaking for myself. I also believe that most people would lean in a similar direction as I do on this concept. That said, I do not speak for everyone. I have no doubt that many people can and will be able to become completely immersed in this adaptation, despite significant character discrepancies. Some may not fit into either camp, and will instead choose to enjoy this adaptation as its own entity, which is also wonderful if that works for you. I don’t want to tell anybody how they should feel, or what kinds of entertainment they should/should not enjoy. My goal was to outline my personal feelings on the impact of significant phenotypic discrepancies on immersion. Which I also happen to believe are commonly felt within the human population at large. But to each their own. And I will certainly not cast any judgment on Paapa’s acting abilities, as I am completely unqualified to do so. He could be the best actor in the world, and my feelings on this issue would still hold true, so it is not specific to him as a person or actor. And thank you again for the recommendation, I will be sure to check it out!

replies.author: @Willy_Warmer
replies.text: @Midwinter2 It's not that we doubt his acting capabilities, it's that the casting just doesn't make sense. Snape is described as pale and sallowy multiple times in the books. Some people in Hogwarts deadass believe he's a vampire. And he's compared to a bat because of how much he stays inside, like he's in a cave (among other things). It is absurdly obvious that Snape was and is a white man, plain and simple. So the fact that they somehow can't get the most immediate and arguably the most important aspect of adapting a book to the big screen down points in every direction to the conclusion that, in all likelihood, they will repeat to come short in other ways. Not to mention the fact that there is literally thousands of white to even white passing actors out there who can and would play Severus Snape---would jump at the opportunity to do so. Yet they passed up on literally all of them for this guy. And, again, maybe he's a great actor. But it's not like he's the best in the world, plain and simple. Nor is he likely the best objective fit for the role. That doesn't mean he automatically should be not cast, but it does mean that if you're passing up all these other people who already fit the role more than him, while he himself almost 100% isn't the "perfect" match or whatever, it implies you have other things on your mind when it comes to the casting unrelated to staying faithful to the source material. And no one wants to watch something that indicates that.

Oh Snape is definitely an antagonist. And a great one at that. He's just not the villain.

Antagonists are plot devices that create obstructions and challenges for your protagonist(and Snape is textbook definition of obstruction and challenges for our heroes), while villains are evil characters with malicious intent.

replies.author: @angusyoung119
replies.text: @TheJohn0363 Look at you, commenting in a Harry Potter thread, trynna be all cool and shit😂😂

replies.author: @kopicat2429
replies.text:
@angusyoung119 Antagonists can do that you though. Their role is to be act as an obstacle, even if that's ultimately for the sake of assisting and helping the Hero, doesn't change their antagonist role. Acting like an obstacle to be overcome usually in order to grow, friend or foe, is what an antagonist is.

That's one of the differences between an antagonist and a villain. Antagonists can be the hero's greatest ally, best friend or their lover. And even want what's best for the MC. It's how they go about achieving that, that categorizes them as an antagonist.

He gives Harry a hard time, he challenges him, pushes him forward. Doing his best to help Harry grow. All the while acting like he hates him. He acts antagonistic towards Harry, even doing so for the sake of helping him doesn't change that dynamic. That's textbook antagonist no?

Edit; At least that's always been my understanding of an antagonist as compared to a villain.

author: @drsch
text:
The casting was a heavy blow. My daughter and I have loved diving into the world of Harry Potter together. I read the books to her, we played Hogwarts Legacy together (after a few mods to fix it), have visited Wizarding World in Orlando twice, and have even produced several stop motion short films using her Harry Potter Lego sets. We were both excited for the HBO series and were looking forward to watching it together. I was even going to get a Max subscription just to watch it. 

No longer. My enthusiasm for this show died. It's clear they're not going to tell the story, they're not going to respect the original work and it's going to be full of modern day social commentary. It's going to be shit. They lost us as fans. 

When I showed my daughter who they were casting, she just about cried in disappointment asking, "why can't they just tell the story?"
